segments.title: Approaching Fushimi-Momoyama Castle
segments.summary: At Fushimi-Momoyama Castle in Kyoto, the visit begins with the climb and an immediate impression of how dramatically the site dominates its surroundings.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke and Glenn introduce their visit to Fushimi-Momoyama Castle while travelling in Kyoto. The approach itself becomes the main historical observation: the castle sits high enough that reaching it is already tiring. Their joking comparison with conquest makes a useful point about how a site can impose costs on any force attempting to attack it. Rather than discussing walls or weapons in isolation, this moment directs attention to the landscape that makes those defenses more formidable. It is a compact watch point for thinking about Japanese castles as positions embedded in terrain rather than simply striking buildings.

evidence.note: The speakers identify the castle, describe the hill climb, and frame the approach as discouraging to a would-be conqueror.

segments.title: Verticality as the castle's first defense
segments.summary: The steep stairs at Fushimi-Momoyama Castle prompt a practical reflection on how elevation, armour, weapons, and fatigue shape an assault.
segments.body: The speakers call the castle's primary defense “verticality,” referring to its long, steep stairways and climb. They then turn the joke into a practical comparison with soldiers of the period. Historical attackers might have had advantages in fitness or familiarity with such terrain, but they would also have carried armour and weapons. That contrast shows why topography matters in siege warfare: a force reaches the defended position tired, burdened, and less able to move freely. The observation does not claim that height replaced fortifications, but it usefully explains how difficult ground can multiply their defensive value.

evidence.note: The speakers describe the endless stairs, name verticality as the primary defense, and compare themselves with armoured soldiers.
